design_philosophy.core_loop: Overhead run-and-gun combat: advance through jungle, shoot enemies in 8 directions, throw grenades upward to clear obstacles and groups, reach end-of-level gate, escape before time expires or hazards kill you
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Infinite enemy waves with limited grenades versus unlimited ammo; navigating tight spaces while managing resource depletion; time pressure in certain level hazards (gas rooms, water)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: One-soldier commando escaping behind enemy lines; Rambo-style jungle warfare where skill and grenade placement overcome numerical disadvantage
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Grenades are the only tool for breaking through obstacles and clearing grouped enemies—forces tactical grenade economy; grenades only fire upward regardless of facing direction, creating deliberate aiming constraints; level design emphasizes prisoner rescue as optional objective, rewarding exploration with hidden bunkers

difficulty.lives_system: Extra lives awarded at 10,000 points and per 50,000 points up to 960,000; thereafter no additional lives. Play continues until all lives exhausted or 140 levels survived
difficulty.continues_system: Continue system present; can resume from end-of-level gate after game over via continue menu with level select cheat access
difficulty.checkpointing: No mid-level checkpoints; end-of-level gate serves as soft checkpoint where waves of enemies stream from fortress; upgrades (machine gun, unlimited grenades, bunker-viewing glasses) are lost upon death, resetting difficulty
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Hidden cheats (invulnerability, timer adjust, level select) accessible via complex button sequences after game over; upgrades found in hidden bunkers grant temporary power but reset on death, creating risk-reward for exploration

progression.upgrade_system: Temporary upgrades found in hidden underground shelters: enhanced machine gun, unlimited grenade refills, glasses revealing all hidden bunkers. All upgrades lost upon death
progression.unlock_mechanism: Bunker locations and upgrades hidden behind destructible walls requiring grenade placement; bunker-viewing glasses unlock visibility of other bunkers; level select cheat unlocks sequential level access after game over
replayability_hooks: Secret ending message triggered by completing game with 3 lives and 24 grenades remaining
Hidden bunkers and prisoner rescues encourage route optimization and replay for 100% completion
Four unique level designs repeated across 16 total levels with increasing difficulty variation
Bonus points for shooting cowardly officer at end-of-level gate encourages aggressive play

world_building_techniques: Jungle setting established via helicopter drop opening and terrain progression
Enemy officer character archetype (cowardly commander) reinforces military hierarchy and comedic tone
Prisoner-of-war rescue mechanic establishes liberation narrative without explicit dialogue
Location names in UI ('BROKE THE ___ AREA NOW RUSH TO THE ___ AREA') suggest geographical progression through war zones

faction_indicators: Super Joe (player character) represents Allied commando
Enemy soldiers and cowardly officer represent opposing military force
Prisoners of war represent civilian/military captives to be liberated
danger_signals: Gas rooms: environmental hazard requiring time-pressure escape
Water obstacles: lethal hazard requiring grenade-based pathfinding
Infinite enemy waves at level gates: overwhelming force scenario
environmental_storytelling: Jungle terrain suggests tropical theater of operations (Southeast Asia implied by geography)
Enemy gate/fortress structures indicate military fortification and hierarchical command
Helicopter extraction framing establishes covert insertion/extraction military operation
POW transport convoys visualize wartime capture and imprisonment mechanics

sources_used: tcrf - Commando (NES) - The Cutting Room Floor (gameplay mechanics, cheat codes, hidden developer messages, regional differences, level/lives system)
wikipedia - Commando (video game) (gameplay overview, designer credit Tokuro Fujiwara, arcade vs NES differences, port information)
